content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Self-Consistency Prompts for LLM Confidence Calibration",
  "problem": "LLMs remain poorly calibrated in their confidence, often overestimating or underestimating uncertainty in their outputs. Existing prompting methods for eliciting uncertainty are brittle and fail to generalize across domains.",
  "motivation": "Reliable uncertainty estimates are critical for applications with high stakes, such as medical or legal domains. As LLMs integrate into decision pipelines, trustworthy confidence calibration is needed to avoid overreliance on incorrect predictions.",
  "method": "We propose 'counterfactual self-consistency prompting': for each input, prompt the LLM to generate multiple answers after minor, controlled perturbations (e.g., synonyms, paraphrases) of the input. The LLM is then asked to provide a confidence estimate based on the observed agreement among its self-generated answers. This method leverages the model's internal consistency under distributional shifts as a proxy for epistemic uncertainty, reducing reliance on ungrounded likelihood scores.",
  "experiment_plan": "Test on NLI (SNLI), QA (SQuAD), and arithmetic reasoning (GSM8K) with standardized calibration metrics (ECE, Brier Score). Baselines: vanilla confidence from logits, majority-vote self-consistency, and chain-of-thought self-consistency. Evaluate whether counterfactual self-consistency prompts improve calibration and uncertainty quantification versus baselines."
}
